macro_segments.title: David's Five Worlds and the Five Instances of 'Barchi Nafshi'

micro_segments.title: Introduction: David's song across five worlds according to Rabbi Yochanan in the name of Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai
First world: David in his mother's womb praising God
Second world: David born and seeing the stars and constellations
Third world: David suckling from his mother's breast and the nature of nourishment
Talmudic discussion on the placement of breasts: understanding versus physical protection
Fourth world: David seeing the downfall of the wicked and praising God
Fifth world: David contemplating the day of death and resurrection

macro_segments.title: The Wonder of Human Creation and Divine Craftsmanship

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon bar Akva's teaching on God's ability to create life within us, compared to human limitations
Discussion of the metaphor: human craftsmen versus divine creation with organs and soul
Explanation of 'Ein Tzur Kelokeinu' as 'Ein Tzaiyar Kelokeinu': God as the sole fashioner
Rav Yehuda bar Menasya's interpretation: there is no way to wear out God, unlike human creation

macro_segments.title: The Soul as a Portion of God: Five Divine Qualities

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon ben Pazi's question: why does David say 'Barchi Nafshi' five times?
The soul fills the entire body as God fills the entire world
The soul sees but cannot be seen, as God sees but is not seen
The soul sustains the entire body as God sustains the world
The soul is pure as God is pure
The soul dwells in the innermost chamber as God dwells in the innermost chamber
Conclusion: Let the soul with these five qualities praise God who possesses them

macro_segments.title: Chizkiyahu's Illness, Isaiah's Visit, and Divine Compromise

micro_segments.title: Rav Hamnuna's teaching: God makes compromise between two righteous people—Chizkiyahu and Isaiah
Chizkiyahu requests Isaiah visit him; Isaiah requests Chizkiyahu visit him—both standing on honor
God brings afflictions on Chizkiyahu; Isaiah is sent to visit him as an act of kindness
Isaiah's prophecy: 'You will die and not live'—meaning in this world and the world to come
Chizkiyahu's complaint: you didn't involve yourself in procreation
Chizkiyahu's reason: he foresaw wicked children through divine spirit

macro_segments.title: Isaiah's Response: 'Don't Worry About God's Hidden Things'

micro_segments.title: Isaiah's famous dictum: these hidden things of God, why are they your concern?
You do your obligation; God does what God wants
Chizkiyahu's response: marry your daughter so our combined merit yields righteous children
Isaiah's refusal: the decree is already made; you won't have time before death
Chizkiyahu's sharp rebuke: end your prophecy and leave
Chizkiyahu's tradition from King David: never abandon hope in God's mercy even at death's door
Rabbi Chanan's teaching: even if a dream-vision says you'll die tomorrow, don't abandon prayer
Chizkiyahu turns to the wall and prays to God
Rabbi Shimon ben Lakish: 'from the walls of his heart'—from the depths of his soul
Levi's interpretation: the Shunammite made one wall; David made the entire Temple

macro_segments.title: Chizkiyahu's Prayer and the Merit of Connecting Redemption to Prayer

micro_segments.title: Chizkiyahu's prayer citing his righteousness and asking God to remember his faithful walk
Yehuda in the name of Rav: Chizkiyahu connected redemption to prayer (Semichas Geulah L'Tefillah)
Discussion of the merit earned through connecting the blessing of redemption to the Shemoneh Esrei

macro_segments.title: Chizkiyahu's Six Reforms: Three Approved, Three Disapproved

micro_segments.title: Introduction: our rabbis taught—six things Chizkiyahu the king did; three approved, three not
First approved reform: hiding the book of healings (Sefer Refuot)
Second approved reform: grinding up the copper snake that Moses made
Third approved reform: dragging his wicked father's bones on a rope bed as dishonor
First disapproved reform: sealing the waters of Gihon
Second disapproved reform: cutting the doors of the Temple and sending them to Assyria
Third disapproved reform: making an extra Nissan month when Nissan had already begun

macro_segments.title: The Book of Healings: Medical Knowledge and Divine Trust

micro_segments.title: Explanation of hiding the book: people relied on it instead of praying to God when sick
Rambam's objection: this contradicts the obligation to heal; doctors must use medical knowledge
Rambam's reinterpretation: the book contained astrological or magical frauds, not real medicine

macro_segments.title: Merit Through Others and Chizkiyahu's Humility

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan in the name of Rabbi Yosei ben Zimra: whoever attributes merit to himself receives it from others
Whoever attributes merit to others receives it for themselves
Moses appealed for Israel's sake; God credited it to Moses himself
Chizkiyahu appealed for his own sake; God credited it to David and not to Chizkiyahu
Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi: even when God sent him peace, it was bitter because it was for David's sake

macro_segments.title: The Shunammite's Room for Elisha: Models of Hospitality

micro_segments.title: Setting: 'Let us make a small wall chamber' for the man of God Elisha
Dispute: was it an exposed upper story that they roofed, or a large portico they divided?
Contents: bed, table, chair, and lamp—Abaye's model of proper hospitality to a sage
Abaye: whoever wishes to benefit from hospitality should follow Elisha's model, not Shmuel's ascetic standard

macro_segments.title: The Shunammite's Recognition of Elisha's Holiness

micro_segments.title: The Shunammite recognizes Elisha as a holy man of God
Rabbi Yosei bar Chanina: women understand guests better than men
Dispute on how she knew: either no flies approached his table, or no nocturnal emission on his linen sheet
Rabbi Yosei bar Chanina: Elisha is holy but his servant Geichazi is not
Geichazi pushed the woman away when she came to tell of her son's death

macro_segments.title: The Reward for Hosting a Sage

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yosei bar Chanina in the name of Rabbi Eliezer ben Yaakov: whoever hosts a scholar and supports him
Scripture considers it as though he offered the daily sacrifice
Transition to practical Halakha: one should stand in a low place to pray, not a high place

topical_tags.term: Barchi Nafshi (Let my soul praise God)
Semichas Geulah L'Tefillah (connecting redemption to prayer)
